mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers digestion and digestive systems (C), with invertebrate digestive systems (C.2.a), vertebrate digestive systems from oral cavity through large intestine (C.2.b), evolutionary development (C.2.c), and biodynamics of secretion control (C.2.d).
Covers basic features of nutrition (A), various nutritional patterns including autotrophism and heterotrophism (A.1), essential nutrients that must be supplied in food (A.3), and syntrophism (A.4).
Substances consumed for nutritional support connect to basic features of nutrition (A), nutritional patterns (A.1), and essential nutrients (A.3).
Photosynthesis as the production of food in green plants (B), converting light energy into chemical energy for autotrophic nutrition.
Plants as autotrophs (A.1) producing food through photosynthesis (B), serving as primary producers in nutritional chains.
The liver as a key organ in the vertebrate digestive system (C.2.b), producing bile for fat digestion and performing metabolic functions.
Algae as autotrophic organisms (A.1) performing photosynthesis as primary producers (B) in aquatic ecosystems.
Fungi as heterotrophic osmotrophs (A.1), absorbing nutrients externally, with mycorrhizal associations exemplifying syntrophic nutritional interrelationships (A.4).
Bacteria exhibit diverse metabolisms including chemotrophism (A.1) and play roles in syntrophic nutritional interrelationships through decomposition and nitrogen fixation (A.4).
Cattle have specialized vertebrate digestive systems with ruminant stomachs (C.2.b) for processing cellulose-rich plant material.
Fish have vertebrate digestive systems (C.2.b) adapted for aquatic nutrition, with gill-based and gut-based absorption.
Insects have invertebrate digestive systems (C.2.a) with specialized feeding structures for diverse nutritional strategies.
Arthropods have invertebrate digestive systems (C.2.a) including tubular systems adapted to diverse feeding modes.
Molluscs have invertebrate digestive systems including the radula for food processing (C.2.a).
Birds have specialized vertebrate digestive systems (C.2.b) with beaks, crops, and gizzards adapted for diverse diets.
Meat as animal tissue consumed for nutritional support, providing essential protein nutrients (A.3).
Vegetables as edible plant parts providing vitamins, minerals, and dietary fiber as essential nutrients (A.3).
Fruits as seed-bearing structures providing nutritional support with vitamins and sugars (A.3).
Maize as the world's highest-volume grain crop providing carbohydrate nutrition (A.3), though limited by low essential amino acids.
Beans as legume protein sources providing essential nutrients (A.3), with nitrogen-fixing plants improving soil fertility through syntrophic relationships (A.4).
Milk as nutrient-rich liquid providing casein proteins, lactose, fats, and calcium as essential nutrients (A.3).
Cooking improves palatability, digestibility, nutrition, and safety, relevant to methods of ingestion and food processing (A.2).
Bread as a baked carbohydrate staple providing essential nutrients (A.3).
Salt provides essential sodium and chloride electrolytes required in diet (A.3).
Hunting as a method of food procurement (A.2), representing a heterotrophic strategy for obtaining animal nutrition.
The human digestive system as a vertebrate digestive system (C.2.b) spanning oral cavity through large intestine.
Digestive hormones coordinate secretion and motility, relevant to biodynamics of the vertebrate digestive system (C.2.d).

Biochemistry characterizes the macromolecule classes (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, nucleic acids) that constitute essential nutrients (A.3) and are substrates of digestive enzymology (C). Its coverage of nutrition applications (deficiency and health) directly supports the essential nutrients content of path A.3.
